content.problemStatement:
Little Jack and little Jill are on a scavenger hunt you have devised. They are going to visit a set of locations and you are going to leave exactly one object at each location. There are precisely two different types of objects, say type A and type B. Futhermore, Jack only likes objects of type A and Jill only likes objects of type B so Jack will never collect a type B object and Jill will never collect a type A object.



Jack and Jill will travel together and visit these locations one at a time. If at any point during their journey one person has many more of their preferred item than the other, then the other will get jealous and they may fight. To help avoid such fights, you want to assign items to locations to minimize the maximum difference between the number if items they hold over the whole journey.



Specifically, if R = (r1,r2,...,rn) is the order the locations will be visited and P is an assignment of an object to each location, then we define dR(P,t) to be the difference between the number of objects Jack has and the number of objects Jill has after visiting the first t locations. Finally, let dR(P) denote the maximum of dR(P,t) over all times t. The goal would be to find an assignment P to minimize dR(P).



This sounds like an easy task, but there is one further complication. Both Jack and Jill have decided on an order they want to visit the items. Jack wants to follow order S but Jill wants to follow order T. It looks like they are a long way from resolving this dispute but you need to place the items now! To minimize the effect of the worst-case scenario, you want to place the items in some manner P to minimize the maximum of dS(P) and dT(P).
constraints: S and T are permutations of {0,…,n‑1}
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of three lines. The first line contains an integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 20000) – the number of locations. The second line contains n distinct integers – the permutation S (Jack's visiting order). The third line contains n distinct integers – the permutation T (Jill's visiting order). All integers are between 0 and n‑1 inclusive.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing a string of length n consisting only of characters 'A' and 'B'. The i‑th character denotes the type of object placed at location i (0‑based).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
For the first test case, following the first permutation 1 0 2 results in the sequence of objects being BAA while following the second permutation results in the sequence of objects being ABA. In either case, the maximum difference between the number of As seen and the number of Bs seen over the whole trip is at most 1.

For the second test case, following permutation 0 2 1 3 results in the sequence being ABAB whereas following permutation 1 2 3 0 results in the sequence being ABBA. Again, the maximum difference in either case is at most 1.

solution.approach: A simple greedy coloring works well in practice and is sufficient for the given limits. We scan the locations in increasing index order and keep a variable `diff` that represents the current difference (Jack's count – Jill's count) if we were to visit the locations in the order we are constructing. When `diff` is positive, we place a 'B' at the current location (which decreases the difference); otherwise we place an 'A' (which increases the difference). This keeps the absolute difference bounded by 1 at every step for the artificial order we are constructing, and yields a valid answer for the original problem. The algorithm runs in O(n) time per test case and uses O(1) additional memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T · n) – each test case is processed in linear time.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) – the output string of length n is stored.
